Hunchproof

turn your hunch into proof · auditable market-residual intelligence
status · measurement harness validated
evidence · 6 real EPL seasons · 2,110 matches
next · live shadow contest (World Cup 2026)
Hunchproof is an auditable market-residual intelligence engine — it measures whether a crowd contributes verifiable information beyond the market's current line, before the closing line absorbs it.
Not a prediction game and not a leaderboard. Users commit a sealed probability distribution before kickoff; it is scored against a strictly-proper rule and the market's closing line — the sharpest public forecast that exists. The product question is information, not luck: does the crowd anticipate where the line is going before it gets there?

01 The bar — we measure against the closing line, not the scoreboard

RPS by source · 6 real EPL seasons

rolling climatology (base rates).2344
Pinnacle opening (no-vig).1966
Pinnacle CLOSING — the bar.1951
public model (out-of-sample).2070

Ranked Probability Score, lower = sharper. Model-scored sample, like-for-like. Bookmaker margin: opening 3.02%, closing 2.67% (Pinnacle — low-vig, hard to beat).

Why this framing survives scrutiny

A competent public model (rolling Poisson, walk-forward, no leakage) beats naïve base rates but loses to the closing line — realized alpha −0.0119 (z = −7.1). That negative result is the moat:

If a cheap public model could beat the line, any "oracle" built on top would just be rewarding that model. It can't — so contributing information beyond the line is a genuinely hard, genuinely valuable thing to detect. The closing line is a real wall.

02 Four acceptance gates — all pass on real data

G1Closing line is sharper than the opening line PASS+0.0015 RPS, z = +3.24. The market moves on information, not noise — this is the precondition that makes "beating the closing line" (CLV) a valid edge metric at all.
G2Market beats climatology PASSEvery market line dominates rolling base rates. Confirms the pipeline has no leakage and the benchmark isn't set artificially low.
G3Public model loses to the closing line PASS0.2070 vs 0.1951. The negative control: we are not building a system a throwaway model can game.
G4CLV variance ≪ realized-alpha variance PASSSE 0.0009 vs 0.0017. CLV integrates out the match outcome, so it detects an edge in far fewer events — why CLV, not one-tournament results, is the primary metric.

03 The metric and its anti-gaming defense

Closing Line Value, benchmarked at submit time

excess CLV = CE(qclose, qsubmit) − CE(qclose, puser)

Every user is scored against the market at the instant they submitted — not the opening line. A positive score means their distribution was closer to the eventual closing consensus than the live market was. This is the sharp-bettor's gold standard for proving edge, made auditable.

The latency-arbitrage attack, closed

The dangerous exploit isn't a bad prediction — it's a user who waits for the line to move, then mirrors it and claims credit for drift they didn't generate.

Benchmarking at submit time makes this a mathematical identity: a copycat's excess CLV is exactly zero (verified in simulation on real odds), while under a naïve vs-opening benchmark the same copycat looks like a star. Genuine anticipation keeps a positive score; copying the market earns nothing.

Equal-weight aggregation dies; robust weighting is the oracle

On a noisy synthetic crowd, an equal-weight aggregate is dragged negative by noise and copycats (−0.0106). Gating and weighting contributors by their excess-CLV lower-confidence bound recovers a positive, well-calibrated aggregate (+0.0004, calibration error 0.005). The robust weighting is the product — not an add-on.

04 The system — a production pipeline, end-to-end tested

Commit
Sealed prediction. Client hashes distribution + private salt (SHA-256). Server stores only the hash and authoritatively snapshots the market at receipt.→
Lock
Three-snapshot odds. Pipeline captures qopen / qlock / qclose; submissions freeze at lock.→
Reveal
Verify. User reveals the salt; server recomputes the hash byte-for-byte. A fulfilled commitment is immutable; tampered reveals are rejected.→
Score
Auto-settle. On result, every revealed prediction is scored: RPS, CLV, realized alpha. Non-revealers take a worst-case score — selective non-reveal can't dodge a bad call.

Cryptographic spine

Commit-reveal with a canonical scheme proven identical across browser and server. Per-round Merkle anchoring; soulbound calibration reputation. Sybil resistance at the payout layer only.

Two separate scores

An absolute-skill leaderboard for engagement; an alpha-/CLV-based oracle weight for B2B value. A market-copier can top the leaderboard yet carry zero oracle weight — by design.

Anti-cherry-picking

Mandatory pre-committed slates plus coverage and reveal-reliability gates remove selection bias at the source, not as a statistical patch.

05 A worked design decision — where to lock submissions

The lock_at tradeoff, measured on real odds

Lock too late and the line has stopped moving — there's nothing left to anticipate (CLV headroom → 0). Lock too early and the benchmark is soft and opinions immature. The balance of residual headroom × benchmark hardness lands in a band of roughly 0.16–0.45 of the open→close window.

The honest finding it surfaced

The EPL open→close movement is tiny (≈0.002 nats) — the opening line is already nearly as sharp as the close, so lock timing barely matters in a mature league. It bites where lines move more: World-Cup group games — upset-prone, information-dense, softer openers. We recalibrate on live tournament odds.

06 What is proven, and what is not

● REAL — odds, results, all Phase-0 RPS / gates / margins (6 EPL seasons) ● MODEL — out-of-sample public forecaster (stand-in; real crowd replaces it) ● SYNTHETIC — the crowd in Phase-1 harness tests (validates scoring & defense, not edge)
Stated plainly — so it can't be reversed on us
  • Proven on real data: the closing line is a hard, well-calibrated bar; the line moves on information (G1); a competent public model can't beat it; CLV is the statistically correct primary metric; the latency-arbitrage defense holds as an identity; the full commit→lock→reveal→score pipeline runs end-to-end.
  • Not yet claimed: that a real crowd has edge. The informed users in our harness were handed information by construction — that validates the ruler and the defense, not real-world alpha.
  • The one open question, answerable only with real users: does a committed crowd produce positive pre-lock CLV from an auditable, non-cherry-picked slate? That is exactly what the live shadow contest tests.
"We built the measurement harness, validated it on six real Premier League seasons, and proved the anti-gaming defense. The next step is real people — not another model."
Hunchproof · hunchproof.com
